coredata.dc:description: Developing high-sensitivity hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensors is crucial. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered one of the ideal materials for developing H2O2 sensors for their adjustable pore size and diverse topological structures. In this work, solvothermal synthesis is utilized CQD@Mn-MOF using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and two-dimensional Mn-MOF ([Mn(Tib)(H2O)3]·(TPA)·3H2O, Tib = 1,3,5-triamidazolylbenzene, TPA = terephthalic acid) as precursors. Interestingly, CQD@Mn-MOF as an electrochemical sensor can effectively detect H2O2 with a lower detection limit of 14.7 μM, and higher sensitivity (approximately 5 times that of pure Mn-MOF). CQD@Mn-MOF sensor is capable of detecting H2O2 with high selectivity in the presence of other interfering agents such as proline, ascorbic acid, glucose, and various metal salts. In addition, CQD@Mn-MOF electrochemical sensor has been used to effectively determine the H2O2 content in actual beverages such as fruit juice and beer. The sensing mechanism has been reasonably analyzed that the MnO2 produced by Mn2+ + H2O2 = MnO2 + 2H+ reaction acts as a specific catalyst for H2O2 decomposition reaction (MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ = Mn2+ + O2 + 2H2O). This sensing mechanism further ensures the high selectivity of the sensor for H2O2. Significantly, the introduction of CQD not only improves the conductivity of Mn-MOF, but also can provide more active sites in the catalytic reaction, improve the contact area and reaction rate of the reaction, and improve the catalytic performance of Mn-MOF, further improve the sensitivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing high-sensitivity hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensors is crucial. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered one of the ideal materials for developing H2O2 sensors for their adjustable pore size and diverse topological structures. In this work, solvothermal synthesis is utilized CQD@Mn-MOF using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and two-dimensional Mn-MOF ([Mn(Tib)(H2O)3]·(TPA)·3H2O, Tib = 1,3,5-triamidazolylbenzene, TPA = terephthalic acid) as precursors. Interestingly, CQD@Mn-MOF as an electrochemical sensor can effectively detect H2O2 with a lower detection limit of 14.7 μM, and higher sensitivity (approximately 5 times that of pure Mn-MOF). CQD@Mn-MOF sensor is capable of detecting H2O2 with high selectivity in the presence of other interfering agents such as proline, ascorbic acid, glucose, and various metal salts. In addition, CQD@Mn-MOF electrochemical sensor has been used to effectively determine the H2O2 content in actual beverages such as fruit juice and beer. The sensing mechanism has been reasonably analyzed that the MnO2 produced by Mn2+ + H2O2 = MnO2 + 2H+ reaction acts as a specific catalyst for H2O2 decomposition reaction (MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ = Mn2+ + O2 + 2H2O). This sensing mechanism further ensures the high selectivity of the sensor for H2O2. Significantly, the introduction of CQD not only improves the conductivity of Mn-MOF, but also can provide more active sites in the catalytic reaction, improve the contact area and reaction rate of the reaction, and improve the catalytic performance of Mn-MOF, further improve the sensitivity.

The CQD@Mn-MOF was synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method with carbon quantum dots (CQD) and the precursor of two-dimensional Mn-MOF (1,3,5-triimidazolyl benzene, terephthalic acid, and manganese acetate). CQD@Mn-MOF can effectively detect hydrogen peroxide using electrochemical sensing technology with a minimum detection limit of 14.7 μM, which is about five times higher than that of pure Mn-MOF, which was also used to effectively determine the hydrogen peroxide content in actual samples such as fruit juice and beer. The results show that the combination of carbon quantum dots and MOF can effectively improve the performance of MOF materials.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: H2O2, as a trace gas, is prevalent throughout the ecosystem [1]. Determination of H2O2 is important in enzymatic reactions and the detection of trace H2O2 is of considerable importance in food safety and environmental applications [2]. Excessive consumption can be harmful to bodily functions and strict control of H2O2 content in food is necessary [3,4]. In the prior art, various methods such as titrimetric, chromatographic, spectroscopic and electrochemical methods have been successfully used for the detection of H2O2 [5].

Over the past few decades, enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors have attracted much attention due to their high sensitivity and excellent selectivity [6]. However, enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors often have the disadvantages of being complex, expensive, and highly dependent on pH and temperature. At present, electrochemical sensing has a faster and more efficient sensing performance for the analysis of different health hazards, making electrochemical sensing one of the superior sensors [7–11]. Non-enzymatic electrochemical biosensors are considered to be very promising candidates for the detection of H2O2 due to their high sensitivity, pH-free stringent requirements, long-term stability, and fast responsiveness [12]. In recent years, transition metals, transition metal oxides or hydroxides, and carbon-based materials have been widely studied as active materials for non-enzymatic H2O2 sensors [13]. However, while high-performance H2O2 sensors are rapidly evolving, their applications are still limited by low toxicity, poor operational stability, or limited electronic conductivity [14]. To address these issues, the development of new high-performance H2O2 detection materials is essential.

As a porous crystal material composed of organic ligands and inorganic metal ions or metal clusters, MOF has entered the field of vision of researchers because of its advantages of high specific surface area, high porosity, high chemical and thermal stability, and has been widely studied and applied in the fields of sensing, adsorption, separation, and energy storage [15,16]. Among them, the layered structure of the MOFs has unique physicochemical properties such as excellent electron transport capacity, large surface area, and high density on the surface to access the active site, as a transition metal layered structure, Mn-MOF exhibits excellent performance in detecting H2O2 through oxidation–reduction reactions. CQDs have received continuous attention from researchers in electrochemical detection due to their high conductivity, enzyme like catalytic activity, excellent stability, easy functionalization, and low cost, especially their synergistic effect with MOFs, which can significantly improve sensor performance [17]. The surface electron density of CQDs is high, and the introduction of CQDs can effectively improve the conductivity of Mn-MOF, optimize the catalytic performance of the material, and further enhance the response of H2O2 sensing [18]. These unique physicochemical properties, such as 2D-MOFs, allow them to be used as electrocatalytic materials for electrochemical sensors [19,20], which was confirmed by the recent work of Zhang and colleagues [21].

In this work, a layered two-dimensional Mn-MOF and its composite CQD@Mn-MOF were prepared by hydrothermal method, and their properties were tested and evaluated by characterization techniques [22,23]. CQD@Mn-MOF acts as an electrochemical sensor for detecting H2O2 in aqueous solution, and the results show that the electrochemical sensor has good anti-interference and sensitivity. The possible sensing mechanism is that MnO2 produced by the reaction of Mn2+ + H2O2 = MnO2 + 2H+ further acts as a specific catalyst for the decomposition of H2O2. Because CQD has a large electron density, combining CQDs and Mn-MOF can effectively improve the conductivity of MOF material, also optimize the electrochemical sensing performance of Mn-MOF for H2O2. This provides a better experimental basis and theoretical guidance for the development of CQD and MOFs.

1,3,5-Triimidazolebenzene (Tib), terephthalic acid (TPA), manganese acetate, sodium chloride, potassium fercyanide, potassium chloride was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urea. Ethylenediamine is purchased from Anagi Chemical Co., Ltd. Ethanol and other reagents are analytical reagent grades, which are provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All analytical-grade reagents are commercially available and require no further purification.

Its performance was tested by scanning electron microscope (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Fourier infrared transform (FT-IR) and other techniques.

The solvothermal method was used to synthesize the Mn-MOF. Accurately weighed solid 1,3,5-triimidazolylbenzene (4.90 mg, 0.02 mmol), terephthalic acid (4.10 mg, 0.02 mmol), and manganese acetate (3.58 mg, 0.02 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solvent of 1.0 mL methanol and 5.0 mL distilled water, ultrasound 5 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 23.0 mL teflon-lined autoclave, sealed and kept at 140 °C for 3 d. After cooling to room temperature, a colorless block crystal, namely Mn-MOF, was obtained by a few times washing with distilled water and filtering.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared according to the method reported in the previous literature [24,25]. Urea (1 mmol) and ethylenediamine (3 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and sonicated for 5 min until complete dissolution. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 23.0 mL teflon-lined autoclave, sealed and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. Cool naturally to room temperature to obtain a brown solution of carbon quantum dots.

The solvothermal method was used to synthesize the CQD@Mn-MOF. First, 1,3,5-triimidazolyl benzene (4.90 mg, 0.02 mmol), terephthalic acid (4.10 mg, 0.02 mmol), and manganese acetate (3.58 mg, 0.02 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solvent of 1.0 mL methanol and 5.0 mL distilled water. 4 μL, 6 μL, and 8 μL of carbon quantum dot solution were added, respectively, and sonicated for 5 min until completely dissolved. Transfer the obtained mixture to a 23.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, heat to 140 °C, and cool to room temperature over 3 days. A small amount of distilled water was washed several times, and colorless bulk crystals with different carbon quantum dot contents were filtered to obtain CQD4μL@Mn-MOF, CQD6μL@Mn-MOF and CQD8μL@Mn-MOF.

The beer and juice samples used in this study were purchased from supermarkets and refrigerated in the refrigerator for later use. Juice and beer samples can be used directly in the simulated ratio without any post-processing.

X-ray crystallography data for Mn-MOF were collected at 100.0 (1) K on a four-ring diffractometer (Rigaku Oxford Diffraction, 2015) using a single-wavelength X-ray source (Cu Kα radiation: λ = 1.54184 Å) on a liquid nitrogen cooler. Data collection and absorption correction were performed using the CrysAlisPro, a program suite with Olex2 [26]. The structure was solved by the SHELXT program [27] and further refined using the SHELXL 2018/3 package [28]. The crystal data of Mn-MOF are shown in Table S1, and the primary bond length and bond angle are shown in Tables S3 and S4.

A three-electrode system was used to test the electrochemical properties of the prepared materials. Among them, the platinum sheet is used as the counter electrode, the saturated calomel electrode is used as the reference electrode, and the composite modified glassy carbon electrode is used as the working electrode (d = 3 mm) [29]. The working electrode modification is mainly divided into the following three steps: First, the glass carbon electrode was polished with three different specifications of alumina polishing powder, placed in absolute ethanol and deionized water, respectively, and then dried naturally in the air [28]. Subsequently, weigh 4 mg of composite material to dissolve and disperse in 0.375 mL of aqueous solution, then add 50 μL of conductive adhesive (Nafion membrane solution) with a concentration of 5 w% and 0.125 mL of ethanol solution, and ultrasonic disperse for 1 h to obtain solution A. Finally, pipette 6 μL of the above solution A, drop coat it on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode, and dry it naturally in the air, and ensure that the composite material is evenly covered on the surface of the electrode and cannot overflow during the coating process (Fig. S5).

Cyclic voltammetry test: The redox activity of electrode materials was studied by cyclic voltammetry curves, and the activity of composites with different carbon quantum dot contents was compared during the identification process. The mechanism and control process of electrochemical detection of H2O2 were analyzed by cyclic voltammetry test, and the potential of H2O2 potentiostatic chronostatic current detection was further determined.

Potentiostatic timing current test: The main electrochemical parameters of the probe during electrochemical detection, including sensitivity, response time, detection limit and detection range, were detected by potentiostat chronostatic current measurement. The operation method was to slowly stir 0.1 M PBS buffer solution, and add different amounts of H2O2 to it one by one to obtain the step current response data, and obtain the current-time curve, further fit the current-concentration curve, and obtain the above main parameters through the i-t curve.

The electrostatic potential of the ligand and hydrogen peroxide was calculated using density functional theory (DFT). The Gaussian 09 W and GaussView 6.0 software packages are used to understand charge structure and distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of Mn-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction analysis of single crystals showed that the Mn-MOF ([Mn (Tib) (H2O)3]·(TPA)·3H2O) belonged to the triclinic crystal system [30], which was located in the Pī space group. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the asymmetric unit of Mn-MOF consists of a Mn(II) ion, a 1,3,5-triimidazolebenzene ligand, three coordination H2O molecules, two crystalline H2O molecules and one molecule crystalline deprotonated terephthalic acid. The central metal Mn forms a hexagohedral coordination octahedral configuration with the surrounding oxygen and nitrogen (Fig. 1b), in which O6, O7, N2 and N6 constitute the plane of the octahedron, N4 and O5 constitute the axial position of the octahedron, and the coordination atoms are three nitrogen from 1,3,5-triimidazolebenzene and oxygen from three H2O molecules, respectively. The bond lengths of Mn-O bonds range from 2.1741(10) Å to 2.2087(9) Å, and the bond lengths of Mn-N bonds range from 2.2004(11) Å to 2.2850(11) Å (Table S1, Table S3 and Table S4). In order to simplify the crystal structure of Mn-MOF, the Mn-MOF was analyzed by topology, with the metal Mn as the node and 1,3,5-triimidazolbenzene as the linker to form the Mn-MOF with a 2D sheet structure, and the whole structure can be simplified to the topology shown in Fig. 1d. Fig. S1a is a SEM image of Mn-MOF, a layered structure of single crystals that accumulates into massive aggregates during growth. As can be seen from Fig. S1b and S1c, there are many small cracks on the surface of the Mn-MOF, but when the carbon quantum dots are loaded into the Mn-MOF, the surface becomes flatter [31]. The EDS spectrum of Mn-MOF (Fig. S1i, Table S2) can clearly observe the signal peaks of C, O, N, and Mn elements, which proves the successful preparation of the material. HRTEM analysis was performed on the composite materials (Fig. S1e, f) and CQD (Fig. S1d), and it can be seen that the CQD@Mn-MOF material has an obvious 2D sheet structure and CQD are approximately spherically uniform distributed, less than 10 nm in diameter. The crystal planes of the composite material were analyzed using SAED mode (Fig. S1g) and stripe structure (Fig. S1h), and matched with the XRD pattern of the material, corresponding to the 002 and 220 crystal planes of Mn-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Powder X-ray diffraction and thermal stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 2 a, the PXRD spectrum was analyzed, and the analysis showed that both CQD and Mn-MOF had good phase purity, and the diffraction peak of CQD was wide. The introduction of CQDs may change the surface energy and interface effect of Mn-MOF. The growth kinetics and crystal face stability of Mn-MOF are affected and leading to changes in the preferred orientation. Meanwhile, the interfacial effect may also lead to enhanced interaction between Mn-MOF and CQDs, further affecting the preferred orientation. Compared with the original Mn-MOF, the diffraction peak of the CQD@Mn-MOF composites appears at the same position, and the peak shape is narrow and sharp, indicating that the composites have the same crystal structure as the original materials and have higher purity. By
D =
K λ
β c o s θ
, where D represents the crystallite size, K is a constant (commonly taken as 0.9), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray (1.5418 Å), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak, and θ is the diffraction angle, we can calculate the crystallite size of the materials. Based on this calculation, we obtained the crystallite sizes of the materials (DCQD = 44.7 Å, DMn − MOF = 824.7 Å, and DCQD@Mn − MOF = 574.3 Å). The largest crystallite size is observed for Mn-MOF, which may indicate that Mn-MOF has better crystallinity. The introduction of CQDs may affect the crystallite size and strain by filling the pores of Mn-MOF or altering its growth mechanism. This could lead to a decrease in crystallite size for CQD@Mn-MOF, while potentially introducing additional strain. The PXRD spectrum of the composite CQD@Mn-MOF was measured after grinding and soaking in aqueous solutions at pH 2, 7 and 12 respectively, and the PXRD spectrum was shown in Fig. 2b, which showed that the CQD@Mn-MOF spectrum was consistent with that before immersion, which proved that the composite had good pH stability and water stability in the pH range of 2 ∼ 12. Fig. 2c is the thermogravimetric analysis curve of Mn-MOF, which lost about 17.23% of its initial weight in the range of 35 °C ∼ 170.83 °C during the heating of the sample to 800 °C, which can be attributed to the departure of crystalline water molecules and coordination water molecules (calculated value of 17.9%). There was no weight loss in the temperature range of 170.83 °C ∼ 404.58 °C, indicating that Mn-MOF was stable in this temperature range. Continued heating from 404.58 °C to 800 °C resulted in a complete collapse of the Mn-MOF frame. The results of thermogravimetric analysis showed that Mn-MOF remained stable below 404.58 °C and showed good thermal stability.

The electrolyte was a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4-, with a sweep speed of 0.1 V· s-1 and a voltage window of -0.2 V ∼ 0.9 V. As shown in Fig. S2a, the results show that the cyclic voltammetry curves of Mn-MOF and CQD@Mn-MOF are significantly different, and the height of the redox peak of the composite CQD@Mn-MOF is significantly higher than that of Mn-MOF. Impedance plots were analyzed with ZView2, and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) values of CQD@Mn-MOF and Mn-MOF were 177.4 Ω and 299.8 Ω, respectively. The smaller the radius of the impedance diagram, the smaller the Rct value, and the better the electrochemical performance. Obviously, compared with Mn-MOF, CQD@Mn-MOF has better charge transfer performance and excellent electrochemical performance (Fig. S2b) [32,33], indicating that carbon quantum dots have been successfully introduced into Mn-MOF. Regarding synthesis CQD@Mn-MOF perform Zeta potential measurement, the Zeta potential value is 9.01 mV (Fig. S2c), indicating that the material surface carries a positive charge, indicating good stability of the composite material [34]. The positive charge on its surface helps to better adsorb H2O2.

The infrared spectra of Mn-MOF and CQD@Mn-MOF are shown in Fig. S3a, and the infrared spectra of the two are only different at 3500 cm-1. The materials and methods used to prepare carbon quantum dots in this paper are completely consistent with those in the literature [35]. In this paper, an infrared characterization of carbon quantum dots has been made. The results show that the carbon quantum dots contain a large number of carboxyl and amino groups, and the expansion peak and vibration peak of -NH also appear at the infrared spectrum of CQD@Mn-MOF at 3500 cm-1 prepared in this paper, which again verifies that the carbon quantum dots have been successfully loaded on Mn-MOF. The peaks corresponding to other positions in the infrared spectrum mainly include: the expansion and contraction vibration peak of the CN bond between 1251 cm-1 ∼ 1369 cm-1. The peak vibration of benzene ring is between 1510 cm-1 ∼ 1624 cm-1. The peaks between 2500 cm-1 ∼ 3421 cm-1 are the expansion and contraction vibration peaks of -OH bonds in crystal water and coordination water and the vibration peaks of carboxyl groups.

Fig. S3b is the X-ray photoelectron spectra of CQD@Mn-MOF materials. The high-magnification scanning spectra of Mn2p on the surface of CQD@Mn-MOF materials were fitted and analyzed (Fig. S3c). The results show that the peaks with binding energies at 653.34 eV and 641.75 eV correspond to the spin orbit doublets of Mn2p1/2 and Mn2p3/2, respectively. The binding energy at the peak at 646.66 eV corresponds to the satellite peak at Mn2p. Fig. S3d-f is the C1s, N1s and O1s high resolution XPS spectra of CQD@Mn-MOF. The C1s spectrum shows the bonding state of CC and CO, indicating that carbon exists in various chemical forms. The N1s spectra reveal the bonding state of NC, N = C, reflecting the chemical environment of nitrogen. The O1s spectra show the Mn-O bonding state. It reflects the chemical composition and electronic state of the material.

In order to study the electrochemical properties of Mn-MOF and CQD@Mn-MOF, the glassy carbon bare electrode (GEC) was modified with materials, and the cyclic voltammetry curves were measured, the reaction device is shown in Fig. S5. Charge transfer occurs on the surface of the electrode. H2O2 decomposition reaction occurs in the reaction system. MnO2 generated in the Mn2+ + H2O2 = MnO2 + 2H+ reaction acts as a catalyst for H2O2 decomposition reaction, and further MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ = Mn2+ + O2↑ + 2H2O reaction occurs to promote H2O2 decomposition, generation of H2O and O2. The cyclic voltametry curve of the GEC bare electrode is shown in Fig. 3 a-c, Mn-MOF modified electrode and CQD@Mn-MOF modified electrode in 0.1 M PBS buffer solution, where the sweep velocity is 0.1 V·s-1. As shown in Fig. 3a-c, the oxidation peak current at 0.8 V of the bare glassy carbon electrode changed slightly with the gradual addition of H2O2 in the potential range of -0.2 V ∼ 0.9 V. However, when H2O2 concentrations of 5 mM, 8 mM, 13 mM, and 18 mM were added to the Mn-MOF modified electrode, the oxidation peak current was significantly enhanced at the electrode potential of 0.8 V, that is, the irreversible redox process of H2O2 oxidation could be observed through a single peak of the electrode in the potential range of -0.2 V ∼ 0.9 V [36]. Cyclic voltammetry images in the presence or absence of H2O2 were analyzed. In the presence of H2O2, a strong and obvious peak value is obtained at 0.8 V, and the oxidation peak current increases gradually with the increase of H2O2 concentration [37]. In the absence of H2O2, there is no obvious peak, which reflects the significant recognition effect of the material on H2O2. Comparing the cyclic voltammetry curves of Mn-MOF and CQD@Mn-MOF modified electrodes, it can be observed that after adding the same concentration of H2O2, the oxidation peak current of the cyclic voltammetry curves measured by the composite modified electrode is more significant, and the increase is significantly stronger than that of Mn-MOF. It can be preliminarily inferred that the composite can be used to detect H2O2 in aqueous solutions.

In order to determine the optimal amount of carbon quantum dots, three composites with different carbon quantum dot contents, CQD2μL@Mn-MOF, CQD4μL@Mn-MOF and CQD6μL@Mn-MOF, were prepared, and the electrocatalytic activity of different samples for H2O2 reduction was evaluated by classical cyclic voltammetry. The results are shown in Fig. 3d, when the amount of carbon quantum dots is 4 μL, the corresponding oxidation peak current intensity decreases when the content of carbon quantum dots is further increased, which may be due to the decrease of electron transfer rate due to the aggregation of carbon quantum dots. The results showed that the optimal amount of carbon quanta was 4 μL for the preparation of Mn-MOF composites.

Then, the effect of PBS (0.1 M) buffer solution (pH 4 ∼ 10) with different pH on the peak current of H2O2 oxidation on CQD4μL@Mn-MOF electrode was tested by cyclic voltammetry. As shown in Fig. 3e, at extreme pH values, the electron transfer rate may decrease, resulting in a weakened current response, and the ability of CQD@Mn-MOF to recognise H2O2 may also change at different pH values. It may have higher selectivity and sensitivity at suitable pH values; while at extreme pH values, the recognition ability will be reduced. In the range of pH 4 ∼ 9, the intensity of oxidation peak current increases with the increase of pH; When the pH > 9, the current decreased significantly, and the results showed that the optimal pH condition for the detection of H2O2 by CQD4μL@Mn-MOF was 9. Therefore, a PBS (pH = 9) buffer solution was selected for subsequent experiments.

The kinetic mechanism of the electrocatalytic identification of H2O2 by CQD4μL@Mn-MOF modified electrode was further explored, and the cyclic voltammetry curves of CQD4μL@Mn-MOF modified electrode were analyzed at different scanning rates, and the electrolyte was 5 mM H2O2 (pH = 9). As shown in Fig. 3f, a pair of redox peaks appeared in the potential range of -0.2 V ∼ 0.9 V, and the peak current increased with the increase of the scan rate. As shown in Fig. 3g, the square root (V1/2) of the oxidation peak current and the scan rate are linearly fitted, and it shows that the oxidation peak current intensity is linearly related to the square root of the scan rate. The results show that the redox reaction of H2O2 on the surface of CQD4μL@Mn-MOF modified electrode is a diffusion control process, which is conducive to the quantitative detection and analysis of H2O2 [38,39].

The electrochemical behavior of H2O2 shows that the Mn2+/Mn4+ redox pair is involved in the oxidation process of H2O2 [40]. The two-dimensional MOF layered network structure is conducive to electron transport, and the loading of carbon quantum dots can effectively improve the electron transfer rate. Mn4+ can be used as an electron transfer medium [41], and the electrochemical oxidation of H2O2 in the presence of oxide Mn4+ causes a great current response in alkaline media. In addition, during the electrochemical reaction, Mn2+ may be converted to Mn4+[42], while Mn4+ is reduced and H2O2 is further oxidized to oxygen and water. The results showed that CQD4μL@Mn-MOF could promote the maximum electrocatalytic activity of H2O2 oxidation under alkaline conditions with the support of redox pair (Mn4+/Mn2+) conversion. Analysis of the high-resolution spectra of Mn 2p before and after the identification of H2O2 (Fig. 3h, i) showed that the electron binding energy of the Mn 2p orbital did not change, ruling out the possibility of H2O2 binding to Mn. Therefore, this excellent performance may be due to the synergy of the material's large porosity, large surface area, and excellent electron transfer network structure.

In addition to the general sensing parameters, the anti-interference performance is another important factor in evaluating the performance of the sensing material. The electrochemically active carbon quantum dots were compounded with Mn-MOF, and the prepared CQD4μL@Mn-MOF showed good anti-interference performance. In order to further investigate the excellent selectivity of CQD4μL@Mn-MOF, H2O2 was added to the PBS (0.1 M) buffer solution at pH = 9, and other physiological substances (such as proline, glucose, ascorbic acid, sodium chloride, sodium perchlorate, calcium chloride, sodium chlorate and pyrosulfite) at a concentration of 0.2 M were added to the buffer solution in turn, and the corresponding current intensity values were recorded to study the anti-interference performance of the sensor [43]. The current response of CQD4μL@Mn-MOF to other analytes (Fig. S4a) showed that the continuous addition of H2O2 analogues had little effect on the peak oxidation current, indicating that CQD4μL@Mn-MOF has excellent anti-interference performance in recognizing H2O2.

The reversibility of the sensor is an important factor in practical applications [44]. The reversibility of the CQD4μL@Mn-MOF electrode was investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The cyclic voltammetry of 1 mM H2O2 in the presence of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 9) at a scan rate of 0.1 V·s-1 showed a significant oxidation peak in the range of -0.2 ∼ 0.9 V (Fig. S4b), and the peak remained stable after a backscan (30 cycles at the same scan rate), indicating good reversibility of the electrode process. In addition, the stability of CQD4μL@Mn-MOF for 5 mM H2O2 was determined within seven days, and the current response of the hydrogen peroxide solution with a concentration of 5 mM was measured by coating the configured CQD4μL@Mn-MOF electrode material solution on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode every 24 h, and the results obtained are shown in Fig. S4c, the current response of the working electrode to H2O2 remained relatively stable for 7 d, and the current intensity could reach 90.8% of the initial current value on the last day. It is proved that CQD4μL@Mn-MOF has good stability as an electrode material.

Applying a potential of 0.8 V (as can be seen from the cyclic voltammetry curve, the current response is strongest with the addition of H2O2 concentration at this voltage), as shown in Fig. 4 a, the current response is stable when different concentrations of H2O2 are added to 0.1 M PBS (pH = 9) buffer solution and stirred continuously, and the stable current occurs about 13 s after the addition of H2O2, indicating that the time required for H2O2 to transfer from the electrolyte to the electrocatalytic site is very short [45,46]. It also shows that CQD4μL@Mn-MOF has good immediacy for the detection of hydrogen peroxide. At the same time, the calibration curves of oxidation current with H2O2 concentration are shown in Fig. 4b, and the results show that both Mn-MOF and CQD4μL@Mn-MOF have a linear range of 0.1 mM ∼ 1.6 mM, and the linear regression equations are I = 1.946 × 10–4C + 1.226 × 10–6 and I = 0.001 C + 1.043 × 10–6, all of which have high linear correlation coefficients (R 2 = 0.99361 and R 2 = 0.99185), and the H2O2 concentration and the corresponding current value show a good linear relationship [47]. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using Eq. (1) and Eq. (2):
(1)
I = 1 + b
[ M ]
(2)
L O D = k × S B / b

Where SB is the blank standard deviation, b is the sensitivity of the method (determined as the slope of the calibration curve), and k is the statistical constant (the widely accepted value is 3) [48].

The lowest detection limits for Mn-MOF and CQD@Mn-MOF were 75.4 μM and 14.7 μM, respectively. In the composites, CQD can be used as an electron donor due to its high surface electron density, which can effectively transfer electrons into the structure of Mn-MOF and significantly improve the conductivity of the composites. Moreover, Mn-MOF itself has excellent electron transfer ability, especially in its two-dimensional hierarchical structure, electrons can migrate more rapidly. The introduction of CQD not only optimizes this electron transport path, but also increases the contact area between the two through their uniform distribution in the Mn-MOF hierarchical structure, further promoting the rapid transfer of electrons. The results show that the sensitivity of Mn-MOF increases about 5 times with the addition of carbon quantum dots. b(CQD4μL@Mn-MOF) > b(Mn-MOF), the sensitivity of the composite material is higher than that of a single Mn-MOF material, which proves that the composite of carbon quantum dots and MOF is an effective method to improve the performance of MOF. As shown in Table S5, in this work, CQD@Mn-MOF exhibits high sensitivity in electrochemical detection of H2O2 and has potential advantages compared to other H2O2 sensors [49–56]. As shown in Fig. S5, the MnO2 produced by Mn2+ + H2O2 = MnO2 + 2H+ reaction is specific to the decomposition reaction of H2O2 and further MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ = Mn2+ + O2↑ + 2H2O reaction occurs to promote H2O2 decomposition, generation of H2O and O2. The overall reaction can be described as H2O2
→
MnO
2
O2 + 2H2O. This sensing mechanism further ensures the high selectivity of CQD@Mn-MOF sensor for H2O2 detection because of the specificity of MnO2 as the catalyst H2O2 decomposition. According to Table S6, the content of Mn determined by ICP is as high as 28.2354%, indicating that this is the main sensing mechanism.

In addition, the electrostatic potential (ESP) calculated by DFT can be used to predict the nucleophilic sites and electrophilic sites more intuitively and vividly. As shown in Fig. 4c we can visualize the distribution of molecular electron clouds [57]. Clearly, the higher sensitivity can be attributed to the electron transfer process at the sensor surface, in which the ligand 1,3,5-triimidazolebenzene functions as an electron donor and H2O2 functions as an efficient electron acceptor, can accelerate electron transfer and assist in the transmission of electrons during sensing processes, as confirmed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations [58]. The sensor exhibits high selectivity for H2O2 compared to common physiology, including proline, ascorbic acid, glucose, and various metal salts. The novel sensor proposed in this experiment provides a simple and efficient electrochemical sensing method for the detection of trace amounts of H2O2 [59,60].

The feasibility of using CQD@Mn-MOF material to detect H2O2 content in real samples of juice and beer was evaluated using the standard addition method. Under optimal conditions, 20 μL of juice or beer was added to 0.1 M PBS with different concentrations of H2O2, and the current response began to occur after about 13 s [61]. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. S6, pure fruit juice and beer were tested and no H2O2 was detected, indicating that the content of H2O2 was lower than the minimum detection limit of CQD@Mn-MOF for H2O2. The recoveries and RSDs were 94.42% ∼ 104.4% and 2.99 ∼ 4.99, respectively. The results proved that the electrochemical sensor was immediate, and feasible for the detection of H2O2 in food.

The formula for calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD) is
R S D = (
SD X
) × 100 %
, where SD is the standard deviation and x is the arithmetic mean, which is commonly used to evaluate the stability and reliability of an analytical method.

In summary, a simple, low-cost, and high-sensitivity H2O2 electrochemical sensor was designed. The two-dimensional porous Mn-MOF modified with different carbon quantum dots was used as the electrocatalytic material to modify the working electrode, which highlighted that the addition of carbon quantum dots significantly improved the specific identification of H2O2 by Mn-MOF, and highlighted the immediacy and feasibility of the sensor for the detection of H2O2. The current was linearly correlated with the H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.1 mM ∼ 1.6 mM, and the minimum detection limit of carbon quantum dot-modified Mn-MOF for H2O2 was 14.7 μM. The sensor also has good selectivity and reproducibility. It can be used for the detection of H2O2 in real food samples for on-site monitoring of food safety. Scheme 1
